questions.question:
In the graph shown below, the depth-first spanning tree edges are marked with a T. Identify the forward, backward, and cross edges.

Graph description: The graph has vertices 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. DFS tree edges (marked T) form the spanning tree. The remaining non-tree edges must be classified as forward edges (from ancestor to non-child descendant), backward edges (from descendant to ancestor), or cross edges (between vertices not in ancestor-descendant relationship).

Note: The graph shown in the image has edges forming a structure where DFS from vertex 1 produces a spanning tree. Based on the image, the graph has vertices connected and DFS tree edges are identified. Non-tree edges are to be classified.
questions.answer: Backward edges: edges from a descendant to an ancestor in the DFS tree. Forward edges: edges from an ancestor to a non-child descendant. Cross edges: edges between vertices with no ancestor-descendant relationship.
questions.to_find: Classify all non-tree edges of the given graph as forward, backward, or cross edges in the DFS spanning tree.

understand.plain: When we run Depth-First Search on a directed graph, the edges we traverse form a DFS spanning tree. The remaining edges (non-tree edges) fall into three categories: backward edges go from a vertex back to its ancestor, forward edges go from a vertex to a descendant that is not its direct child, and cross edges connect vertices that have no ancestor-descendant relationship.

keywords.explain: The set of tree edges traversed during a DFS that connect every visited vertex to its parent in the DFS recursion.
keywords.example: If DFS visits 1->2->4, then edges (1,2) and (2,4) are tree edges.

keywords.explain: An edge (u, v) where v is an ancestor of u in the DFS tree. It creates a cycle.
keywords.example: If DFS tree has 1->2->3 and there is an edge (3,1), then (3,1) is a backward edge.

keywords.explain: An edge (u, v) where v is a descendant of u in the DFS tree but v is not the direct child of u.
keywords.example: If DFS tree has 1->2->3 and there is an edge (1,3), then (1,3) is a forward edge.

keywords.explain: An edge (u, v) where v is neither an ancestor nor a descendant of u in the DFS tree.
keywords.example: If DFS tree has 1->2 and 1->3 as separate branches, edge (2,3) is a cross edge.

understand.visual_alt: A graph with 5 vertices where DFS tree edges (marked T) form a spanning tree. Non-tree edges are classified as backward, forward, or cross edges based on their relationship to the DFS tree.

given.aim: Classify all non-tree edges in the DFS spanning tree as forward, backward, or cross edges.

terms.meaning: An edge traversed during DFS that connects a vertex to a newly discovered vertex.
terms.example: Edge (1,2) if 2 is discovered through 1 during DFS.
terms.connects: These are marked T; all remaining edges need classification.

terms.term: Discovery time d[v] and finish time f[v]
terms.meaning: Each vertex v gets a discovery time when first visited and finish time when DFS backtracks from it.
terms.example: If DFS visits 1, then 2: d[1]=1, d[2]=2.
terms.connects: Used to classify edges: if d[u] < d[v] < f[v] < f[u], then (u,v) is a forward/tree edge; if d[v] < d[u] then (u,v) is backward.

terms.meaning: Edge (u,v) is a cross edge if f[v] < d[u], meaning v was fully processed before u was even discovered.
terms.example: If vertex v is in a different subtree and already finished, the edge from u to v is cross.
terms.connects: In undirected graphs, cross edges cannot exist; in directed graphs they can.

given.plan: Run DFS on the graph, assign discovery/finish times to each vertex. For each non-tree edge (u,v), classify it: if v is an ancestor of u in DFS tree it is a backward edge; if v is a descendant (not direct child) it is a forward edge; otherwise it is a cross edge.

steps.title: Understand edge classifications using DFS timestamps
steps.formula_raw: For edge (u,v): backward if d[v] < d[u] < f[u] < f[v]; forward if d[u] < d[v] < f[v] < f[u] and not tree edge; cross if f[v] < d[u]
steps.apply: In DFS, when we explore edge (u,v) and v is already visited: if v is gray (still in stack / on current path), it is a backward edge. If v is black (fully finished) and d[u] < d[v], it is a forward edge. If v is black and d[v] < d[u], it is a cross edge.
steps.note: In an undirected graph, all non-tree edges are backward edges. In a directed graph, all three types can exist.

steps.title: Apply to the given graph
steps.apply: From the image, the DFS spanning tree (T edges) connects the vertices as shown. The non-tree edges of the directed graph must be examined: edges going from a vertex to an ancestor in the DFS tree are backward edges; edges from a vertex to a non-child descendant are forward edges; edges connecting vertices in different DFS subtrees (already finished branch) are cross edges. The specific classification depends on the exact graph structure shown in the image.
steps.note: This is a GATE 1989 question where the graph is drawn with specific DFS tree edges marked. Students need to visually trace the DFS tree and classify each remaining edge.

solution.result: Non-tree edges are classified as: Backward edges (to ancestor), Forward edges (to non-child descendant), Cross edges (between unrelated subtrees).

questions.formula_note: DFS edge classification theorem: in DFS of a directed graph, non-tree edges are classified as back, forward, or cross edges based on ancestor-descendant relationships in the DFS tree, determined by discovery and finish times.

questions.question:
The most appropriate matching for the following pairs is:

X: depth first search     1: heap
Y: breadth first search   2: queue
Z: sorting                3: stack

A. X-1, Y-2, Z-3
B. X-3, Y-2, Z-1
C. X-3, Y-1, Z-2
D. X-2, Y-3, Z-1
questions.to_find: Match each algorithm (DFS, BFS, sorting) to the data structure it primarily uses (heap, queue, stack).

understand.plain: This question asks us to match three algorithms with the data structures they rely on internally. Depth-First Search uses a stack (or recursion which is an implicit stack), Breadth-First Search uses a queue to explore vertices level by level, and efficient sorting algorithms like Heap Sort use a heap (priority queue).

keywords.explain: A graph traversal that goes as deep as possible before backtracking. Uses a stack (explicit or call stack via recursion).
keywords.example: In a maze, DFS keeps going down one path until a dead end, then backtracks.

keywords.explain: A graph traversal that explores all neighbors at the current level before going deeper. Uses a queue to maintain the order of exploration.
keywords.example: BFS from vertex A explores all vertices at distance 1, then distance 2, etc.

keywords.explain: A complete binary tree satisfying the heap property, used in heap sort and priority queues.
keywords.example: Heap sort builds a max-heap from an array and repeatedly extracts the maximum.

x='70' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Stack</text><text x='70' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>LIFO</text><rect x='150' y='100' width='120' height='50' rx='8' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three columns showing DFS->Stack, BFS->Queue, Sorting->Heap as the correct matching.

given.aim: Find the correct matching between the three algorithms and the three data structures.

terms.meaning: Graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking.
terms.example: DFS on a tree visits the leftmost path all the way down before visiting siblings.
terms.connects: Internally uses a stack (recursion stack or explicit stack) to remember the path.

terms.meaning: Graph traversal that explores all vertices at the current depth before moving to the next depth level.
terms.example: BFS finds shortest path in unweighted graphs by exploring level by level.
terms.connects: Uses a queue to maintain the order of vertices to be explored.

terms.meaning: Arranging elements in order; Heap Sort specifically uses a heap data structure.
terms.example: Heap Sort achieves O(n log n) by building a max-heap and repeatedly extracting the maximum.
terms.connects: The heap data structure is the key to O(n log n) comparison-based sorting.

given.plan: Recall which data structure each algorithm fundamentally relies on: DFS uses stack (LIFO for backtracking), BFS uses queue (FIFO for level-order traversal), and Heap Sort uses a heap (for efficient maximum extraction).

steps.title: Match DFS to Stack
steps.formula_raw: DFS iterative: push start; while stack not empty: u = stack.pop(); for each neighbor v: push v
steps.apply: DFS uses Last-In-First-Out behavior to backtrack. When implemented iteratively, it explicitly uses a stack. When implemented recursively, the call stack serves this purpose. So X (DFS) matches 3 (stack).
steps.note: The LIFO property of a stack naturally implements backtracking behavior needed by DFS.

steps.title: Match BFS to Queue and Sorting to Heap
steps.formula_raw: BFS iterative: enqueue start; while queue not empty: u = dequeue(); for each neighbor v: enqueue v
steps.apply: BFS uses First-In-First-Out behavior to explore vertices level by level, which is exactly what a queue provides. So Y (BFS) matches 2 (queue). Heap Sort uses a max-heap to repeatedly extract the maximum element in O(log n) time, giving O(n log n) overall. So Z (sorting) matches 1 (heap). This gives X-3, Y-2, Z-1.
steps.note: Option B (X-3, Y-2, Z-1) is the correct answer.

solution.result: B. X-3, Y-2, Z-1 (DFS uses Stack, BFS uses Queue, Sorting uses Heap)

questions.formula_note: DFS uses a stack (LIFO) for backtracking; BFS uses a queue (FIFO) for level-order traversal; Heap Sort uses a heap (priority queue) for O(n log n) sorting.

questions.question:
Let G be an undirected graph. Consider a depth-first traversal of G, and let T be the resulting depth-first search tree. Let u be a vertex in G and let v be the first new (unvisited) vertex visited after visiting u in the traversal. Which of the following statements is always true?

A. {u, v} must be an edge in G, and u is a descendant of v in T
B. {u, v} must be an edge in G, and v is a descendant of u in T
C. If {u, v} is not an edge in G, then u is a leaf in T
D. If {u, v} is not an edge in G, then u and v must have the same parent in T
questions.answer: C. If {u, v} is not an edge in G, then u is a leaf in T
questions.to_find: Determine which statement about vertex v (first new vertex visited after u) is always true in a DFS of an undirected graph.

understand.plain: In DFS of an undirected graph, when we visit vertex u, we then try to visit its unvisited neighbors. If v is the very next new vertex visited after u, either v is a neighbor of u (and {u,v} is a tree edge), or u has no more unvisited neighbors (u is a leaf in the DFS tree) and we backtrack to some ancestor and then visit v from there. The key insight is: if {u,v} is NOT an edge, then u must have had no unvisited neighbors — meaning u is a leaf in the DFS tree.

keywords.explain: The spanning tree formed by the edges traversed during DFS. Each vertex has a parent (the vertex from which it was first discovered).
keywords.example: If DFS visits A, then B from A, then C from B, the tree edges are (A,B) and (B,C).

keywords.explain: A vertex with no children in the DFS tree, meaning it had no unvisited neighbors when visited.
keywords.example: A vertex with degree 1 in G is always a leaf in the DFS tree if its only neighbor was already visited.

keywords.term: First new vertex visited after u
keywords.explain: The very next vertex that DFS marks as visited after marking u as visited. This could be a neighbor of u (direct tree child) or a vertex visited after backtracking from u.
keywords.example: If DFS is at u and all neighbors of u are visited, DFS backtracks and visits the next unvisited neighbor of u's parent.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='220' rx='10' fill='#F8F7F2'/><text x='15' y='25' class='tag mut'>Case 1: {u,v} is an edge (v is child of u)</text><circle cx='80' cy='70' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>p</text><circle cx='80' cy='140' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='80' y='146' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>u</text><circle cx='80' cy='200' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='206' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>v</text><line x1='80' y1='88' x2='80' y2='122' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='158' x2='80' y2='182' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='3'/><text x='95' y='175' class='tag ok'>T</text><text x='15' y='215' class='sub mut'>v is child of u in T</text><text x='230' y='25' class='tag mut'>Case 2: {u,v} not an edge (u is leaf)</text><circle cx='280' cy='70' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='280' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>p</text><circle cx='280' cy='140' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='280' y='146' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>u</text><circle cx='370' cy='140' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='370' y='146' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>v</text><line x1='280' y1='88' x2='280' y2='122' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='298' y1='70' x2='352' y2='130' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='240' y='195' class='sub mut'>u is leaf; v is sibling (child of p)</text><text x='240' y='210' class='sub no'>u has no unvisited neighbors!</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two cases: Case 1 where v is a direct child of u (edge {u,v} exists), and Case 2 where u is a leaf with no unvisited neighbors, so DFS backtracks and v is visited from a different ancestor.

given.aim: Determine which statement about the relationship between u and v in a DFS traversal is always true.

terms.meaning: An undirected graph where edges have no direction, so {u,v} = {v,u}.
terms.example: A social network where friendships are bidirectional.
terms.connects: In undirected graphs, DFS non-tree edges are always backward edges; cross edges don't exist.

terms.term: v = first new vertex after u
terms.meaning: The next unvisited vertex that DFS discovers immediately after visiting u.
terms.example: If DFS visits u then immediately marks v as visited, v is the 'first new vertex after u'.
terms.connects: If {u,v} is an edge, v is u's child in T. If {u,v} is not an edge, DFS must have backtracked.

terms.meaning: A vertex u is a leaf in T if it has no children in T, i.e., all neighbors of u in G were already visited when DFS reached u, or u has no neighbors.
terms.example: A vertex with all neighbors already colored gray/black when DFS visits it becomes a leaf.
terms.connects: This is the key: if {u,v} is not an edge, u could not have directly discovered v, so u must have had no unvisited neighbors — u is a leaf.

given.plan: Analyze each scenario: either {u,v} is an edge (v is a direct DFS child of u) or {u,v} is not an edge (DFS backtracked from u, meaning u had no unvisited neighbors, so u is a leaf in T). Then check each option.

steps.title: Analyze what happens when v is the next new vertex after u
steps.apply: After DFS visits u, it tries to visit an unvisited neighbor of u. If u has an unvisited neighbor, it visits that neighbor next — and that neighbor would be v, connected by edge {u,v}. If u has NO unvisited neighbors, DFS backtracks up the tree to find the next unvisited vertex, which would be v. In the second case, {u,v} is NOT an edge in G.
steps.note: So {u,v} being an edge means v is discovered directly from u; {u,v} not being an edge means DFS backtracked from u.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply:
Option A: 'u is a descendant of v' — FALSE. If {u,v} is an edge, v is visited AFTER u, so v is a descendant of u, not the other way around.
Option B: 'v is a descendant of u' — not ALWAYS true. If {u,v} is not an edge, v is visited after backtracking from u, so v is NOT a descendant of u.
Option C: 'If {u,v} is not an edge, then u is a leaf in T' — TRUE. If DFS visited v as the next new vertex without going through u, DFS must have backtracked from u (u had no unvisited neighbors), making u a leaf in the DFS tree.
Option D: 'u and v must have the same parent in T' — FALSE. After backtracking from u, DFS may go several levels up before finding the next unvisited vertex.
steps.note: Option C is the only statement that is ALWAYS true. The backtracking mechanism of DFS is the key.

solution.result: C. If {u, v} is not an edge in G, then u is a leaf in T

questions.formula_note: DFS backtracking property: DFS only backtracks from a vertex when all its neighbors have been visited, making that vertex a leaf in the DFS tree. In undirected graphs, non-tree edges are always backward edges.
